title: CS50 2D - Lecture 4 - Super Mario Bros.
summary: CS50 2D Lecture 4 teaches platformer game development using Lua, covering tile maps, animations, procedural level generation, collision detection, and game entities. The course is offered through Harvard University via edX and other platforms with open access.
writeup: CS50 2D Lecture 4 walks through building a Super Mario-style 2D platformer from scratch using LOVE2D (Lua), progressing from static tile grids to a fully playable randomly generated world. The lecture introduces the five core systems that make a platformer feel like a real world: tile maps, frame-by-frame sprite animation, procedural level generation, platformer-specific collision detection, and a minimal enemy AI. The through-line is that each mechanic, from collidable terrain to gem-containing blocks, reduces to a 2D integer array plus a lookup rule, the same data structure used for Match 3 in the prior week.
key_points: A tile map is a 2D integer array where each cell holds a tile ID (in this implementation, 5 for sky and 3 for ground), and the renderer loops over it to draw 16x16 pixel quads from a tile sheet, making the entire game world a data structure rather than hand-placed art.

Camera scrolling is implemented by calling love.graphics.translate with a negative X offset tracked to the player, so all draw calls shift together without each object needing its own camera-aware coordinate.

Sprite animation differs from a static moving rectangle: a walking Mario cycles through distinct frames per step, and a jumping Mario switches to a separate pose, requiring an animation state machine that advances frames on a timer.

All levels are procedurally generated in code rather than hand-authored, with randomized backgrounds, tile sets, gaps, and block contents (some blocks hold gems, some are empty), so no two playthroughs share a layout.

Platformer collision detection departs from symmetric AABB: the player resolves vertical collisions differently from horizontal ones so that landing on top of a block and running into its side produce the correct distinct responses.

The enemy (a snail standing in for a Goomba) uses a minimal AI: it moves in one direction and reverses on hitting a wall or a level edge, illustrating that even simple directional logic produces convincing obstacle behavior.
